data.title: Alibaba: Buy This Little Dip
data.description:
My Thesis Update

I was fortunate to initiate my coverage of Alibaba Group (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) stock at the end of January 2024, during its lowest local price point. So in a way, I luckily managed to catch the local bottom for BABA stock at the time, and since then, it has grown by 51.5%, whereas the S&P 500 index (SPX) has increased by almost 22.4%. In my last bullish update on BABA, when the stock had only risen 8.8% since I began my coverage, I called for buying the dip as I believed Alibaba remained one of the most undervalued yet highly attractive opportunities available at that time. I saw a promising catalyst on the horizon that could "represent a turning point for Alibaba and the entire Chinese stock market." And so it happened - China's party leadership announced massive measures to stimulate the economy, enabling Chinese equities to make a huge comeback. Against all this backdrop, BABA stock couldn't help but react with a sharp price rise:

Recently, however, we have seen the stock's sharp rise followed by a slight decline - the relief of the rally began in early October and continues to this day, with BABA stock down about 14.5% off its 2024 high:

However, I don't believe that this relief will last long: In my opinion, BABA should continue to rise as investors return to China and the economy and the company's diverse business advantages show signs of a sustained recovery. The upcoming Q2 numbers shall provide an additional catalyst to convince investors that BABA deserves buying. Hence, my "Buy" rating today, with a call to buy this little dip.

My Reasoning

Alibaba Group's Q1 FY2025 report (calendar Q2 2024) revealed mixed signals, with modest growth and resurgent challenges. The firm generated RMB 243.2 billion in revenue, a 4% growth over last year, but below the RMB 246 billion consensus forecast. In US dollars, revenue came in at $33.94 billion, which is also up 4% YoY but falls short of the $34.6 billion expected. Non-GAAP earnings per share of RMB 2.05 was down 9% YoY, but normalized $2.29 per ADS beat the consensus of $2.09, according to Seeking Alpha Premium. So we could see that the economic problems of China - such as high unemployment and declining consumer demand - continued to hit Alibaba's main e-commerce business. On the other hand, Wall Street analysts started to revise their forecasts for Q2 FY2025 more actively, which is generally a good sign:

Breaking down the segments' performance, we see that the Alibaba Chinese retail business (including Taobao and Tmall Group) went down by ~1% to RMB 107.4 billion, now accounting for ~47% of total revenue. This was mainly due to a 9% decline in direct sales despite a 1% increase in customer management. Adjusted EBITDA for this business declined 1% to RMB 48.8 billion, and EBITDA margin slightly rose to 43.1%. The strategic shift from a user-centric perspective and pricing wars have put pressure on margins, which typically fell between 40-60%. However, Alibaba's management now expects that higher GMV will eventually increase revenue and margins. Prospective estimates are what we should care about the most, as the stock prices usually reflect the forwarding projections, not the past results.

The international commerce segment remained Alibaba's star, with revenues up 32% to RMB 29.3 billion year-on-year, fueled by a 38% increase in international retail sales. Yet, this area recorded a negative margin of -12.7% which is a drastic decline from -1.9% at the same time last year, but I believe as the GMV expands, the margins should follow eventually.

Alibaba Cloud, another major area of interest for us, experienced a 6% growth in revenue at RMB 26.6 billion. Alibaba Cloud is focusing on increasing revenue quality by eliminating lower-margin contracts and taking advantage of the strong demand for public cloud and AI solutions. We already see some initial successes: Cloud's EBITA jumped 155% YoY, and margins increased from 3.6% to 8.8%. I believe more to come, as the Western peers tend to earn times more in terms of margins (Amazon's (AMZN) AWS makes 38% in EBIT margin, for example).

In spite of these growth opportunities, in the last few years, Alibaba has struggled a lot, especially in the e-commerce sector. It's been falling short in market share to its peers such as PDD Holdings (PDD) and Douyin, who have penetrated into Alibaba's longtime areas of dominance: beauty and fashion. Between March 2022 and March 2023, Alibaba's market share in China's online retail sales of merchandise declined from 72% to 62%, according to Morningstar Premium data (proprietary source, August 2024). This is due to increased competition and the move to Taobao, which has a lower take rate than Tmall. In the long term, these competition pressures will likely drive down the company's monetization rates. But I think the GMV growth should smooth out the negative effect over time. In addition to that, the cloud opportunity, as well as the international commerce's marginality expansion should make BABA's financials stable enough for its management to keep rewarding the shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Alibaba's balance sheet looks quite healthy, in my opinion, with high cash resources and a strong cash flow mix from its e-commerce stores. The company is announcing a large share buyback program that it expects to expand by $25 billion to $35.3 billion over the next 3 financial years. This move is in line with the wider strategy to drive ROIC and remit more capital to shareholders. So this way, based on my calculations, Alibaba may repurchase nearly 15% of its market capitalization from the market over the next 3 years. Coupled with a small yet stable dividend of over 1%, this strategy should provide a comfortably attractive total shareholder return.

I believe Alibaba's economic moat, the combination of a strong network effect and operational expertise, hasn't been demolished in this competitive environment. The company is still monetizing its platform well and still has higher adjusted EBIT margins than other players such as JD.com (JD) and Pinduoduo.

For fiscal Q2 FY2025, BABA forecasts a gradual alignment of its core China commerce revenue with GMV growth. Alibaba also predicts strong revenue growth in the cloud, which will be driven by AI-related and core public cloud demand. The company is also geared towards streamlining its services and improving cross-selling between public cloud and AI solutions. So despite the difficult macroeconomic conditions in China, which are now expected to improve over the next few years, Alibaba remains determined to improve and capitalize on growth in the cloud and outside China.

Despite its strong growth over the past several weeks, the BABA stock remains quite undervalued, in my view. Although its free cash flow to market cap ratio (i.e., FCF yield) has declined from ~16% at the beginning of the year to ~9.4% today, the total cash on the company's balance sheet still exceeds 35% of its market cap. This suggests that the market continues to underestimate the company and remains skeptical of its recovery potential.

Meanwhile, if you examine Seeking Alpha Quant rating concerning Alibaba's Valuation, you'll notice it has shifted over the past 3 months indeed (while it's not overly negative, BABA has become slightly more expensive). However, according to the key valuation metrics I focus on (I highlighted them below), it remains quite undervalued. For instance, the forward PEG ratio, which accounts for P/E weighted on future EPS growth rate, indicates an undervaluation of nearly 28%. Additionally, the company's forwarding EV/EBITDA is 34% below the sector median. So I believe Alibaba would need to grow by about this amount to eliminate its margin of safety.

I think we might see an additional catalyst in the form of the company's fiscal Q2 report (scheduled for release on November 15, 2024) to see the recovery's continuation. Analysts are expecting BABA's EPS to decline by almost 5% YoY, according to Seeking Alpha data. Furthermore, recent changes in EPS estimates suggest that Wall Street has become increasingly cautious about the company's EPS growth prospects through the end of fiscal 2025:

I believe that, given the positive internal factors I've outlined above, the current environment is favorable for Alibaba to exceed analysts' forecasts in Q2 at least. In my opinion, this could serve as a positive catalyst, potentially leading to a continued recovery in the medium term.

Risks To My Thesis

One risk lies at the macroeconomic level. The incentives recently announced by the Chinese Communist Party are a strong catalyst for market growth, as they're expected to boost retail investor activity in the mainland's stock market and stimulate the real estate sector. However, it's important to remember that the debt burden in the Chinese real estate sector remains a major problem. So those incentives may only address the symptoms and not the causes of the economic challenges. If this is the case, I don't believe that the negative effects plaguing the Chinese economy will disappear anytime soon.

The second risk relates to strong competition and Alibaba's role in its niche markets. While the company's broad diversification and growth in certain segments paint an overall positive picture, it can't be ignored that Alibaba continues to lose market share to faster-growing Chinese companies in its key areas. If this trend continues, its stock price could stagnate or even fall further.

I also come to the conclusion that the company is undervalued. In my opinion, however, undervaluation alone is not a sufficient reason to buy the stock. Alibaba has remained cheap over the last two years, but this has not prevented the stock price from falling further and becoming increasingly undervalued. So there is a risk that this trend may continue.

Your Takeaway

Despite the numerous risks mentioned above, I believe that investors should consider adding to their positions in Alibaba stock after it has fallen slightly since the beginning of October. Although the company is indeed facing difficult times, I believe it has a clear plan for developing its key segments, increasing GMV, and improving profitability in areas that are currently unprofitable or marginally profitable. Given the scale and moat of the company and the significant investment it is putting into its further growth, I believe it's inevitable that management will succeed in these efforts. This should result in the fundamentals eventually becoming a driving force for the stock's growth in the medium term. The undervaluation that I identified about 3 months ago has decreased slightly but is still significant enough to warrant investors' attention. Buying the stock today could still provide a margin of safety, in my view.

With all of this in mind, I believe the stock will continue to rally over the medium term, and the company's fiscal second-quarter report could serve as a catalyst for that rally, especially if it beats forecasts as I expect it.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Staying The Course With Northwest Bancshares As Earnings Near
data.description:
Usually when I write a company a ‘hold’, it is because I expect performance to more or less match the broader market for the foreseeable future. A good example of this playing out can be seen by looking at Northwest Bancshares (NASDAQ:NWBI). Back in July of this year, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. Even though I was tempted to upgrade it, I felt as though it was still too early to do so. Since this decision, the stock has risen by 5.1%. That's not terribly different from the 4.2% increase experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

To those looking at the fundamentals, this increase might seem peculiar. After all, in the most recent quarter for which data is available, the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, the enterprise reported earnings that fell far short of what they were last year. However, when you look deeper and understand why earnings declined, and you adjust for that, things do look better. To be perfectly honest with you, even with this adjustment, shares are not yet worthy of an upgrade. But that could always change. In fact, new data, covering the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, is expected to come out before the market opens on October 29th. Analysts currently forecast a revenue increase year over year. But with profits likely to decline, I see little hope for an upgrade absent a major outperformance by the company.

Dissecting Northwest Bancshares

I think a good place to start when it comes to looking at Northwest Bancshares would be to cover its most recent financial figures. For the most recent quarter, which is the second quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, the business reported net interest income of $107.2 million. That's an increase of 7.6% compared to the $99.6 million the company reported one year earlier. So far, things there are looking nice. However, this is largely because of provisions for credit losses for both loans and unfunded commitments. If we ignore this, we would actually get a slight decline from $108.5 million to $106.8 million. Even though the company enjoyed an increase in total interest earning assets, it also saw an increase in interest-bearing liabilities. In addition to this, its net interest margin contracted from 3.28% to 3.20%. This is not terribly shocking in this environment. Many banks that I have looked at over the past two years now have seen some weakness on this front, as high-interest rates have led to banks competing with one another over depositors.

The trouble for the business, however, involves its non-interest income. Year over year, this metric fell from $29.8 million to negative $8.8 million. This brought results for the first half of this year down to $19.1 million compared to the $53.8 million reported one year earlier. At first glance, this probably looks rather disastrous. However, there are certain onetime items in here that investors should adjust for. Most notably, during the most recent quarter, the company booked a $39.4 million loss on the sale of investments. That compares to an $8.3 million loss that was offset by an $8.3 million gain on the sale of mortgage servicing rights booked one year earlier. Adjusting for all of this, we actually would get non-interest income that would be considerably higher than what was posted.

As a result of this weakness, net profits in the second quarter of this year totaled only $4.7 million. That's down precipitously from the $33 million reported the same time last year. This brought total net income for the first half of this year down to $33.9 million, compared to the $66.7 million reported for 2023. If we adjust for the one-time items that I mentioned, however, net profits in the most recent quarter would have been $29.1 million. That's up a hair from the $29 million we would get on an adjusted basis for the second quarter of last year. And for the first half of this year, we would get an increase to $56.2 million compared to the $52.7 million reported at the same time of 2023.

No discussion of a bank can be had without looking at some changes in its balance sheet. During the most recent quarter, Northwest Bancshares booked deposits of $12.09 billion. Unfortunately, the institution is not in growth mode. This is evidenced by the fact that deposits in the first quarter of this year came in at $12.07 billion. That's barely higher. At least I can say that uninsured deposit exposure still remains below the 30% maximum threshold that I tend to prefer. It's currently 25%. But if you make certain adjustments like removing those that have certain collateral to them, you do get this number down to 11.5%. From the first quarter of this year to the second quarter, the value of loans actually dropped from $11.38 billion to $11.23 billion.

Other aspects of the balance sheet deserve attention as well. In the second quarter, Northwest Bancshares had $1.81 billion worth of securities. This was a drop from the $1.90 billion reported for the first quarter of the year. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents grew, they say from $119.3 million to $228.4 million. The good news is that this increase in cash did not come at the cost of having higher debt. In fact, debt declined over the course of the last quarter from $644.7 million to $486.4 million. That's a pretty nice decline.

If we annualize the adjusted earnings for this year, we would get net profits for 2024 in its entirety of $126.9 million. Unfortunately, this would result in a price to earnings multiple of 13.6. As you can see in the chart above, I compared Northwest Bancshares to five similar firms using this approach. Only two of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is, indicating that our candidate is around the middle of the pack. However, the 13.6 reading that we get is higher than the 6 to 10 range that I typically like to see. But this is not the end all be all. If the company is trading at a substantial discount to book value, that could make up for it. Unfortunately, that is not the case either. In the table below, you can see you both the price to book and price to tangible book multiples for not only Northwest Bancshares, but also for the five institutions I am stacking it up against. In both cases, Northwest Bancshares ended up being cheaper than these other firms. But on an absolute basis, it's a bit more expensive than I would like it to be when considering its price to earnings multiple as well.

The last thing that could justify an upgrade for the company would be if its asset quality was high enough. But sadly, that doesn't appear to be the case either. Using the adjusted figures that I calculated previously, we get return on assets of 0.88%. In addition to not being terribly impressive, this is also lower than all but one of the five companies I compared the business to. This can be seen in the first chart below. And in the subsequent chart, you can see the same thing when it comes to return on equity. Once again, only one of the five companies ended up being cheaper than our candidate is. And with a return on equity of 8.15%, our candidate is not that impressive even in a vacuum.

Near the start of this article, I did mention that earnings data will soon be coming out. That should occur on October 29th, before the market opens. At present, analysts are forecasting revenue of $140.1 million. This would represent a slight increase over the $138.3 million reported one year earlier. Earnings per share, however, are forecasted to drop from $0.31 to $0.26, which would bring net income for the company down from $39.2 million last year to $33.1 million this year.

Takeaway

As things stand, I have to say that I am not the most impressed when it comes to Northwest Bancshares at this point in time. It is certainly not the worst bank that I've seen. But it also doesn't strike me as one that warrants significant upside. That's why, at best, I can really only rate the company a ‘hold’ for now. But if something does change when results come out for the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, my mindset on the matter could change.

data.title: Tesla Q3 Earnings: Not As Bad As Expected
data.description:
Article Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) reported its most recent earnings results on Wednesday afternoon. The company beat profit estimates, showing a small year-over-year improvement. There were other positives as well, although Tesla nevertheless does not look like a great investment here. The valuation remains very elevated, which is why I will continue to avoid TSLA.

Past Coverage

I have written about Tesla here on Seeking Alpha in the past. My most recent article is from July, when I covered the EV company's weak Q2 results. I gave the company a Sell rating back then, which has worked out well so far, as Tesla currently trades below the price one quarter ago, despite the broad market rallying since then. With one quarter having passed and with Tesla reporting its next earnings results today, it is time for an update.

What Happened?

Tesla, Inc. reported its most recent earnings results, for its fiscal third quarter, on Wednesday afternoon, following the market's close. The headline results can be seen in the following screencap from Seeking Alpha:

We see that the company managed to beat profit estimates, which was a surprise -- the company had missed profit estimates for a couple of quarters in a row before the Q3 report. On the other hand, Tesla missed revenue estimates by around 2%, showing a year-over-year gain of close to 8% -- not bad, but not excellent, either.

Shares reacted positively to this announcement, trading up 12% at the time of writing. It looks like the market reacted positively to Tesla's better-than-expected profitability during the quarter, while the positive cash flow numbers may also help explain the market's positive reaction.

Tesla's Q3 Report: Things To Like And To Dislike

Let's delve into the details of Tesla's third-quarter earnings report. We already knew what Tesla's delivery numbers looked like, as those are reported before the earnings release each quarter. But despite delivery numbers being known, analysts had overestimated Tesla's ability to generate revenues. The company managed to grow at a high single-digit pace, while analysts were looking for a revenue increase of around 10% compared to the previous year's quarter.

Tesla's revenue generation was very uneven, with the core automobile business delivering a meager 2% revenue increase. Considering inflation is still running north of 2% right now, Tesla's biggest business unit by far is thus not generating any real growth, which is not a good sign.

The global electric vehicle market has not grown at a strong pace this year. This is due in part to a weak EV market in Europe, but the absence of any real revenue growth from Tesla's auto business is still a bit disappointing. After all, Tesla is not at all priced like an “average” automobile company -- for a company that trades at around 100x net profits, the biggest business unit should deliver considerable growth in nominal and in real terms, I believe.

The fact that vehicle volumes were up 6% compared to the previous year's quarter, while automobile revenues rose by just 2%, is also an issue. Average selling prices clearly are coming down, despite the introduction of the high-priced Cybertruck, which should impact average selling prices positively. The fact that this was not enough to reverse the negative ASP trend could indicate margin issues for the automobile business down the road. Demand also seems to have been relatively weak, as indicated by the relatively meager volume growth despite price reductions, while the company's growing inventory levels point in the same direction.

Global inventory levels rose to 19 days of supply, which is up from 16 days of supply one year earlier -- a roughly 20% increase. If demand was very strong, supply would shrink, especially since Tesla has been lowering its vehicle prices. But if inventory levels keep climbing despite pricing incentives, demand unfortunately can't be very strong. Macro uncertainties might play a role, but rising competition from Chinese EV players could also be a factor. Finally, it is possible that CEO Elon Musk's pro-Republican positioning may have alienated some potential customers.

Thankfully, a weak automobile business was partially balanced out by a much stronger performance from the energy business. Here, revenues rose by a strong 52% compared to the previous year's quarter, while the energy business also saw its gross margin expand to around 30%, up by a strong 600 base points on a sequential basis. The strong revenue growth rate for the energy business was largely the result of a great energy storage performance. The company hit a record high when it comes to Powerwall deployments, while total storage deployed rose to 6.9GWhs, which is up by a massive 75% on a year-over-year basis.

The energy business can be quite lumpy, so there is no guarantee that the business unit will perform equally strong during the fourth quarter and beyond. However, at least during the third quarter, the energy business did quite well on both a business growth and a financial perspective -- sales volumes were attractive, and so were the unit's margins.

On a company-wide basis, Tesla saw its margins expand compared to one year earlier, largely due to the nice performance of the energy business and due to higher regulatory credit revenues. Regulatory credit revenues are very high-margin, thus growth in this metric can influence profitability quite a lot. Q3 was the second-best quarter in terms of regulatory credit sales, which helps explain Tesla's ability to beat profit estimates with the Q3 report.

Tesla's operating margin rose to 10.8%, which is better than what Tesla achieved in the last couple of quarters, although an operating margin in the 10%-11% range is still not overly high compared to other automobile companies: BMW (OTCPK:BMWYY), for example, achieved a 10.8% margin over the last year as well, while volume manufacturer Toyota (TM) achieved a 12% margin over the same time frame. Tesla thus looks solid, but far from spectacular from a margin perspective right now.

Between some revenue growth and some margin growth, Tesla's earnings per share climbed 9% on a year-over-year basis, rising from $0.66 to $0.72. At the current level, Tesla earns a little less than $2.50 annualized. Bullish investors seem to believe that this warrants a share price well exceeding $200, although others will disagree with that -- but that's what makes a market.

While cash flow generation had been an issue in the recent past, with free cash flow being negative during the first half of the year, that changed during Q3: With the help of changes in operating assets and liabilities that boosted operating cash flows by $1.6 billion, Tesla generated strong quarterly operating cash flows of a little more than $6 billion. Free cash flows were in the $3 billion range, which was a marked improvement versus the first half of the year. That being said, Tesla has now generated cumulative free cash flows of $1.5 billion in 2024, or around $2 billion annualized. For a company that is valued at around $700 billion, that's not much, as this pencils out to a free cash flow yield in the 0.3% range.

Liquidity is not a concern, however, as Tesla has a pretty strong balance sheet with a $34 billion cash position. Tesla thus has ample reserves to invest in its robotaxi efforts, its energy business, and so on.

Is Tesla A Good Investment?

Tesla's Q3 report was better than expected and showed several positives: Profits improved, margins recovered to some degree, and Tesla's cash flow performance also was a lot better than the pretty bad numbers from the first half of the year.

That being said, Tesla also continues to have problems: Average selling prices continue to shrink, despite the growth from the high-priced Cybertruck, and Tesla's automobile business is not faring well, with no real (inflation-adjusted) revenue growth and with growing inventory levels.

Tesla has potential in the future robotaxi market, but it does not have any product on the road yet, unlike peers such as Waymo (GOOG) (GOOGL). It is hard to say whether Tesla will be able to catch up with the current market leaders.

Tesla's annualized free cash flow yield currently stands at just 0.3%. With shares being valued at around 100x earnings while many competitors trade at a fraction of that, Tesla is too expensive for me and I continue to see shares as quite risky. Even if Tesla's shares were to fall by 20% or 30%, for example, they would be far from cheap, after all. Tesla is showing relatively weak auto growth right now, thus in a recession or economic downturn it could suffer meaningfully. Overall, I thus remain bearish on Tesla, mainly due to its valuation and massive competition in both the EV and the robotaxi market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why I'm Avoiding Annaly Capital After Earnings
data.description:
Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NYSE:NLY) just released its third quarter earnings. The release missed estimates on net interest income (NII), GAAP earnings and earnings available for distribution (“EAD,” a metric similar to adjusted funds from operations, or “AFFO”). The company managed to avoid a dividend cut (an issue recently), but EAD was barely enough to cover the current dividend, resulting in a 98.4% payout ratio for the quarter. So, the risk of future dividend cuts is still present.

The fact that so much of Annaly's return comes from its distribution is a major draw, especially with retirees, who often prefer regular dividends over capital gains because they see the former as “safer.”

Unfortunately, Annaly Capital isn’t the type of dividend stock/REIT that gives dividend stocks their reputation for safety. It is, rather, an ultra-high yield REIT with a long-term track record of reducing its dividends. Such stocks often deliver inferior total returns, as Annaly itself has done over the last five years. Despite the ultra-high yield, the stock’s five-year total return was only 7.22%, less than 2% compounded annually.

Unfortunately, it seems like there is a serious risk of Annaly Capital underperforming in the future. Although the company’s focus on agency mortgage backed securities reduces the risk of truly cataclysmic events (e.g., bankruptcies), this “agency backing” does not protect the company from lower yields on variable rate debt due to the Fed’s rate cutting. Annaly Capital mostly invests in fixed rate mortgages, but some are variable rate, as described in the company’s “diversified capital management” statement. Furthermore, the company's Series F Preferreds (NYSE:NLY.PR.F) are variable rate, and therefore impacted negatively by the ongoing rate cutting.

The last time I covered Annaly Capital, I rated the REIT a hold because of its poor dividend track record and the availability of alternatives with similarly high yield but better financial management. I also floated the idea of buying the company’s preferred shares in place of its equity because said preferreds, being variable rate, gained from the interest rate hikes that were taking place at the time. Although I still have roughly the same opinion on Annaly as I had before today's earnings release, the release made me doubt whether even the preferred shares are worth holding. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will explain why I have made this reversal of preferences in light of Annaly's recent earnings release, while also counselling investors to tread carefully with Annaly securities in general.

Earnings Recap

In the third quarter, Annaly Capital delivered:

  • $13.4 million in net interest income, down 75% q/q but up from a loss y/y.
  • $0.05 in EPS, up from a $0.09 loss (a miss).
  • $0.66 in EAD per share, down 3% q/q, unchanged y/y.
  • $0.65 in declared in dividends, unchanged.
  • A 12.95% return on equity (“ROE”).
  • A 1,300% reported earnings-based payout ratio.
  • A 98.4% EAD based payout ratio.

Some comments on these earnings are in order, since they materially impact my thesis on Annaly Capital. The company's earnings metrics broadly declined on a year-over-year basis, with a few being unchanged. The slight decline in EAD from the previous quarter caused the company's payout ratio to increase compared to the trailing 12-month payout ratio (97%). Finally, the dividends on the preferred shares increased, reflecting less cash available to pay dividends to common shareholders. While the rising preferred dividends might seem on the surface to incentivize buying those shares instead of NLY equity, I don't think that's a great long-term bet either, as I'll explain in subsequent paragraphs.

The Macro Now Favors the Equity (in Theory)

While I’m not especially bullish on any Annaly Capital securities, the macro environment broadly favors the common shares right now, rather than the preferreds. The reason is that the majority of the company’s balance sheet is invested in fixed rate securities, while the preferred shares are a variable rate instrument. In times of falling rates, fixed rate assets perform better than variable rate assets, as they give investors access to yields that are higher than those on newly issued loans in the lower rate regime. The higher yield on high fixed rate debt is usually recognized and “priced in” pretty quickly. However, if you’re holding fixed rate debt before a period of interest rate cuts, you earn a higher return than the holder of variable rate debt, whose interest payments decline.

Now, what actually happened in the third quarter ran contrary to the theoretical point above. Preferred dividends increased, common share dividends remained unchanged. Nevertheless, if the Fed continues cutting rates, things should eventually play out as the above paragraph would predict.

As the “agency” page on Annaly’s website points out, the majority of the company’s holdings in its agency MBS segment are fixed rate. Unfortunately, the company doesn’t break down exactly what percentage of its loans are fixed rate vs. variable rate. However, its earnings presentations do break down the size of each segment by asset value. In the company’s second quarter supplementary info, the “agency MBS” segment is shown as having $64.39 billion in assets, compared to $67.08 billion for the company as a whole. Recall that the “agency” page on the corporate website describes the assets in this segment as fixed rate. This fact, combined with the agency segment making up 95.9% of total assets, implies that fixed rate loans make up nearly 96% of Annaly’s asset base.

In theory, Annaly’s common shares should benefit from this, while its Series F Preferred shares should not. Since an overwhelmingly fixed rate loan book funds Annaly’s dividends, the company’s dividends should theoretically remain fixed in the years ahead. As I’ll show in a later section, this isn’t exactly what has happened historically, but it should happen if Annaly’s managers manage their costs and risks intelligently.

It’s a very different story with the Series F preferreds. Although these preferreds are backed by the same fixed rate assets that make up most of Annaly’s portfolio, they have a variable rate structure built into them. Specifically, the rate is SOFR plus 4.993%. The spread is big enough that even if we somehow enter a 0% interest rate regime, investors will still get about a 5% yield. However, if they buy for a 10.5% yield and watch that yield decline to 5%, they are likely to be disappointed. On the other hand, the fixed rate loans on Annaly’s portfolio should enable management to keep the dividend on their common shares stable, if they manage their costs and risks well. So, ignoring all concerns about Annaly’s management competence, its common stock looks more appealing than its series “F” preferreds now.

Why I Don’t Like Annaly Common Stock Much

Although the macro environment presently favors Annaly’s equity over its preferreds, there are company-specific reasons to avoid NLY equity. One of those is management’s track record.

The present trend of rate cutting by the Federal Reserve is very positive for Annaly’s portfolio. The company has a 5.17% yield on earning assets. Further, the homeowners supporting these mortgages are in reasonably good shape on average, with a mere 0.57% delinquency rate nationwide. The company just has to keep its cost structure unchanged, to keep paying a fixed payment to shareholders. Furthermore, as rates continue to decline, these fixed payouts become more valuable, which provides Annaly with the ability to sell some of its assets at substantial capital gains. So if Annaly’s management executes, their company’s dividend should either stay where it is now or rise.

The problem here is that Annaly’s management team has not executed especially well historically. The company’s income statements show large losses on asset sales in three of the last fiscal years. The dividend has decreased by about 10% per year over the last five years. Despite the dividend cuts, the stock’s payout ratio still approaches 99%. Finally, the company achieved its largest-ever dividend payments way back in 2009, which speaks to a very, very long period of decline in subsequent years. Despite the somewhat favorable macro climate, investors can’t rule out the possibility of more dividend cuts here. The company has the macroeconomic wind at its back, but its management keeps dropping the ball.

Valuation

Finally, we get to the factor that is most favorable to Annaly, and the reason I’m rating it a hold rather than a sell:

The multiples.

Annaly’s common shares are fairly cheap. As illustrated by the table below, they trade at just 7.6 times earnings and 1.05 times book. Both of these are cheaper than Annaly’s sector.

Furthermore, Annaly Capital pays so much out in dividends that it can actually afford more dividend cuts: the yield is so high that the stock ends up being worth it even after a decline! NLY pays $0.65 in dividends per quarter, or $2.60 per year. Let’s imagine that Annaly’s team mismanages their operation and the dividend has to be cut to $2 per year. That’s a substantially lower dividend than the dividend being paid out today, specifically 23% lower. However, $2 discounted at a 10% discount rate is worth $20, which is ever so slightly higher than Annaly’s current stock price. If you perform this same exercise while using Annaly’s current dividend, assuming that it remains stable, then you get a $26 fair value estimate–considerable upside. In other words, this REIT is so cheap right now it might just be worth it, despite management’s track record not being great.

The Bottom Line

When I last covered Annaly Capital, I said that I wasn’t interested in the equity because of its high risk in the rising rate environment we were in at the time. I said that I preferred its preferred shares for the same reason: they gained from rising rates. Today, the situation has reversed, and I can’t recommend any Annaly Capital security. I will say, though, that the common shares are now cheap enough that they can afford another dividend cut or two and still be worth the investment. Shorting this stock would be risky.

data.title: Northern Trust Corporation 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Northern Trust Corporation in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Boston Scientific Q3: Good Growth, But Not Good Enough To Justify Its P/E Ratio
data.description:
Introduction

Boston Scientific Corporation (NYSE:BSX) announced Q3 results, which saw decent growth y/y but further margin deterioration. I wanted to take a look at the number in more detail and give some comments on the company's outlook. In my opinion, the low double-digit top-line growth and deterioration in margins don't justify the company's rich valuation; therefore, I am not in a rush to start a position but will monitor what is going to happen with the acquisition, the trials, and approvals over the coming quarters.

Q3 Numbers

The company's top-line growth came in at 19.3% y/y and stood at $4.21B, which beat estimates by $170m. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.63, which beat estimates by 4 cents.

Going into a little more detail, the MedSurg revenue segment, which includes Urology, Endoscopy, and Neuromodulation, saw a growth of around 10% y/y. The Cardiovascular Segment, which includes Cardiology and Peripheral Interventions, grew at an astounding 25% y/y driven mostly by sales in the Cardiology segment.

Moving on to profitability and efficiency, gross margins came in at around 69%, essentially flat y/y. Operating margins came in at 17.4% compared to 19.6% in the same period last year. SG&A seems to have grown faster than the top line, at around 25% y/y, which led to a slight decrease in profitability overall. Adjusted gross margin came in at 70.4%, which was slightly lower than the management anticipated, primarily driven by foreign exchange.

In terms of the full-year outlook of '24, the company expects top-line growth to be around 16.5%, and GAAP EPS to be in the range of $1.28 to $1.3, and $2.45 to $2.47 on an adjusted basis. These full-year numbers are about in line with what the analysts have expected, so no surprise there.

Currently, the company's shares are slightly down as of writing this update and recovered quite a bit since the bottom of $83 a share. At one point, the shares were down over 5% due to the FARAPULSE study update, which said that the company is going to temporarily pause it due to unforeseen complications. It could be that, or it could be general jitters in the markets that brought all the indices down today.

In my opinion, operationally, it was a mixed bag. Commendable top-line growth of 19%, offset by slight margin contraction and the complication of the study, which I think contributed minimally to the way the share price has progressed this morning.

Comments on the Outlook

I want to see the revenue growth accelerating, as well as margin improvements. The management says they expect the second half of the year to have higher gross margins than the first half; however, it will still be below 2023 levels, which is not ideal, in my opinion. In terms of revenues, I would have liked a higher growth rate than what the company is projecting, which, as we saw, is around 16.5%. This is a company that has been appreciating dramatically over the last year, and with a P/E ratio of 71 and a FW P/E ratio of 36, I expect higher growth rates and improving profitability, not the other way around. The market has high expectations for the company for it to trade at such a high P/E ratio. Analysts expect the company to achieve low double-digits in top-line growth while a little higher in adjusted EPS, but I don't think the company should be trading at such estimates.

A lot of the company's performance depends on regulatory approvals and trials. Currently, the company has the upcoming OPTION trial, which will be presented at the American Heart Association Conference on November 16th and could present some short-term volatility, either positive or negative, depending on the data gathered. This trial is critical in proving the safety and effectiveness of the WATCHMAN FLX Pro. The same goes for the recently announced SIMPLIFY Trial, which is designed to assess the safety and efficacy of a less intensive post-procedure drug regimen for people who receive the WATCHMAN FLX Pro.

When it comes to FARAPULSE, approvals in Japan and China have been welcomed, and the company expects to launch it in 2025, which will have a meaningful impact on the business. China is a big region, so I would like to see what kind of revenue it will generate for the company eventually. In terms of the pause of the AVANT GUARD trial, these things happen, and to accurately compile all the necessary data, all the variants have to be accounted for. The management still expects FARAPULSE to perform well.

In terms of acquisitions, the management mentioned that its guidance doesn't include the results of Axonics, which hasn't closed yet and is due to close at the end of the year. This has been a strategic move by the company to expand its urology portfolio. There is a lot of growth opportunity in this segment, and with Axonics top line growth of 34% in 2023, it will be highly accretive to BSX's top line. However, due to amortization expenses and acquisition-related charges, there is a chance that the company's GAAP earnings will see further deterioration. In time, I am expecting all these issues to ease, and margins should improve. Back when the acquisition was announced, the management expected to close the deal by the first half of '24. Thus, I don't know how confident I am that the company will finally close the deal by the end of the year. We will see if anything is going to change in the next quarter.

What I'm doing with this information

The question is, how confident are you that there will be an acceleration in revenues and improvement in margins over the next while? In my view, the market is valuing BSX too richly right now; therefore, I am in no hurry to start a position. The revenue growth rates are too low to justify a P/E ratio of 70, while the management still expects further deterioration in margins, at least in the short term.

I think Boston Scientific Corporation is priced to perfection, and any meaningful miss in the upcoming few earnings reports may force some people to take profits. The company's share price has steadily increased by 75% in the last year and 51% YTD, which I don't think the company's performance justifies. BSX is on my watch list, and I will monitor what is going to happen with the trials and approvals in the next while.
